overview: A tool, colloquially known as f*ck, with a Rust core and web UI to enable non-technical users to perform complex, graph-based data merging. The system is architected for high performance using a lazy-evaluated engine (Polars) orchestrated by a demand-driven incremental computation framework (Salsa) to provide a highly reactive user experience.

goals.primary: Enable non-technical users to independently merge and clean complex, messy tabular data with confidence.
goals.secondary: Drive viral adoption through an exceptionally intuitive and powerful user experience.
goals.technical: Create a robust, lazy-evaluated engine that uses Salsa for incremental computation to provide near-instantaneous feedback on query changes.

targetAudience.description: Users in roles like Marketing or Sales Ops who are proficient with spreadsheets but lack coding skills for advanced data manipulation.
painPoints: Existing tools are either too simple or too complex.
Manual data merging is slow, error-prone, and not repeatable.

userStories.title: Merge customer data from three CSVs for a marketing campaign
steps: User uploads multiple source files to a visual canvas.
System scans files and detects data types, including mixed types in a single column.
User defines destination schema via drag-and-drop, including setting primary keys.
User maps source fields to destination fields by drawing links.
User is prompted to set merge policies (e.g., 'First Match') for conflicting fields.
A real-time preview drawer shows resolved and unresolved records with explanations.
User can inspect any merged value to see all source values that contributed to it.
User downloads the final merged file and can save the mapping 'recipe'.
userStories.technicalImplementation: The react-flow state (nodes and edges) is converted into the `QueryPlan` JSON format on every change (debounced for performance). This JSON is sent over a WebSocket to the Rust backend. The backend's query compiler translates this into a Salsa-tracked operation graph. The engine executes the graph to generate a preview (e.g., first 50 rows), streaming results back over the WebSocket to render in the UI's preview drawer.

userStories.title: Automate a data cleaning pipeline via CLI
steps: Developer creates a `query.json` (potentially exported from the UI) to define the merge logic.
Developer runs the engine via the command line: `f-ck --query query.json --output final.csv`
The engine lazily processes the data and streams the output to a specified file.
userStories.technicalImplementation: The CLI (`f-ck-cli`) uses `clap` to parse arguments. It reads the `query.json`, deserializes it into the `QueryPlan` struct, and passes it to the `f-ck-core` engine. The engine executes the full query (not just a preview) using its Polars and Salsa backend, and streams the resulting DataFrame to the specified output file using the `DataWriter` module.

architecturalDecision.summary: The core engine will be architected according to **Pattern B: The Salsa-Wrapped Execution Approach**, as detailed in the 'Architectures for Embeddable, Incrementally-Updated Analytical Engines in Rust' report. This pattern uses the Salsa framework to manage the execution of a high-performance query engine like DataFusion or Polars.
architecturalDecision.justification: This architecture is the optimal choice for an interactive, user-driven tool like f*ck. It provides an ideal balance of developer ergonomics, high performance, and reactivity. The primary benefits are: 1) It allows the use of familiar and expressive SQL/DataFrame APIs from Polars/DataFusion. 2) The complexity of dependency tracking and cache invalidation is handled automatically by Salsa. 3) The on-demand, pull-based nature of Salsa is a perfect fit for a UI where computation is a direct response to a user's action, as opposed to a continuous stream of incoming data.
architecturalDecision.implementationNotes: The implementation will involve modeling base data sources as Salsa input queries (e.g., `#[salsa::input] fn file_contents(db: &dyn Db, path: PathBuf)`). A chain of derived, tracked queries will then perform the processing steps, such as parsing files into DataFrames (`#[salsa::tracked] fn parsed_dataframe(...)`). A top-level tracked query will define the final analytical computation, taking all necessary parsed DataFrames as dependencies and executing the full merge query using DataFusion/Polars. Salsa's automatic memoization will ensure that unchanged DataFrames are reused across executions, providing coarse-grained incrementality.

alternativesConsidered.reasonForRejection: While providing the most efficient fine-grained incrementality, this approach requires expressing all logic within a dataflow model. This represents a significant paradigm shift and is better suited for continuous, streaming workloads rather than our interactive, request-response model.

This pattern introduces an additional layer of complexity by adding a federated query engine. While powerful, it is not necessary for our core use case, which focuses on tabular data sources. It would be a candidate for future expansion to heterogeneous data sources.

dslEvolution.summary: To effectively leverage Salsa's incremental computation, the user-facing DSL (`QueryPlan`) needs to be treated as a 'logical plan'. This plan will be compiled into a more granular, internal 'physical plan' or 'operation graph' that Salsa can track.
dslEvolution.proposedStrategy: Implement a 'Query Compiler' within `f-ck-core`. This component will take the user's `QueryPlan` and translate it into a graph of granular operations (e.g., LoadSource, LinkKeys, ApplyMergePolicy). Each node in this graph will correspond to a Salsa tracked function. When the user changes the `QueryPlan`, only the affected nodes in the internal graph will be recomputed.
dslEvolution.impactOnArchitecture: This strategy is a direct implementation of our chosen 'Salsa-Wrapped Execution' architecture. It creates a clean separation between the user-facing API and the internal query execution representation, which is essential for achieving our performance goals.

coreLibraries.polars: The core data manipulation engine for executing query steps.
coreLibraries.salsa: For incremental computation and caching of the query graph.
coreLibraries.axum: For the web server framework and WebSocket handling.
coreLibraries.petgraph: For building the entity resolution graph for transitive closure.
coreLibraries.serde: For serializing and deserializing all JSON communication.

crateStructure.name: f-ck-core
crateStructure.desc: Contains all business logic: DSL definitions, the query compiler, Salsa database setup, and Polars plan construction.

crateStructure.name: f-ck-server
crateStructure.desc: The Axum web server. Handles WebSocket connections, manages user sessions, and calls into `f-ck-core`.

crateStructure.name: f-ck-cli
crateStructure.desc: The CLI entry point. Uses `clap` for parsing and calls into `f-ck-core`.

clientToServer.payload: The full `QueryPlan` JSON object. Sent whenever the user's mapping changes.
{ offset: number, limit: number }. For lazy-loading the preview table.

serverToClient.payload: JSON representation of a slice of the resulting DataFrame.
Schema and stats for a newly uploaded source file.
